aliases: 参峇鱿鱼 (Shēn Bā Yóu Yú)

chinese: 参峇鱿鱼 (Shēn Bā Yóu Yú)

food_subgroup: Stir-fried squid with sambal chilli paste; cooked composite dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for sambal squid. Closest parent concept: stir-fried squid dish (FOODON:03302757 — squid food product). No exact match available as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Squid and cuttlefish — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for sambal sotong; sambal preparation not separately coded in FoodEx2 as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJK covers squid/cuttlefish as a food category. The sambal preparation layer is not separately represented; this is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for sambal sotong or sambal squid dish as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker serving of sambal sotong is approximately 150g, comprising squid rings or whole baby squid coated in sambal chilli paste, typically ordered as a side dish with steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal Sotong — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central squid nutrient profile (SR Legacy #15149)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) and micronutrients anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo sambal sotong entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy squid (mixed species, raw, #15149) adjusted for sambal preparation. Sodium elevated to reflect belacan and sambal paste contribution. Values represent the composite cooked dish per 100g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood dish data; University of Sydney GI Database reference for protein-dominant seafood preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed estimation
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal sotong in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on the food's macronutrient profile: 17g protein/100g, 3.5g fat/100g, and only 3g carbohydrate/100g. High protein and fat content significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (40 × 3.0g available carbohydrate per 100g × 1.5) / 100 = 1.8g, classified as very low. This estimation methodology is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database analogous-food principles for high-protein seafood dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from belacan and sambal paste
hypertension.guidance: A 150g serving of sambal sotong contributes approximately 450mg sodium, primarily from belacan (fermented shrimp paste) and sambal chilli paste; individuals with hypertension should account for this within a daily sodium target of 2000mg and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes such as salted fish or preserved vegetables.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic load and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal sotong has an estimated GI of 40 and a glycemic load of only 1.8 per 150g serving, making it one of the most diabetes-friendly hawker side dishes available; its high protein content (25.5g per 150g serving) supports satiety and glycemic control when paired with a moderate portion of rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol from squid offset by very low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid contains approximately 200mg cholesterol per 100g (300mg per 150g serving), which exceeds older dietary thresholds, but saturated fat is very low at 0.8g/100g; current cardiovascular guidelines (AHA 2021) prioritise saturated fat reduction over dietary cholesterol restriction, making sambal sotong acceptable in moderation for most CVD patients, though those with familial hypercholesterolaemia should seek individual dietary advice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Sotong is a quintessential Singapore hawker and Malay seafood side dish consisting of tender squid rings or whole baby squid wok-tossed in a rich, fiery sambal chilli paste. The sambal is prepared by blending dried chillies, shallots, garlic, and belacan (fermented shrimp paste), then sautéing the paste until fragrant and the oil separates — a technique known as pecah minyak — before adding the squid and simmering briefly. The dish delivers a bold combination of spicy, savoury, and slightly sweet flavours, often brightened with tamarind juice for tanginess. Texture-wise, the squid is characteristically firm and slightly chewy, contrasting with the glossy, aromatic sambal coating. Nutritionally, sambal sotong is an outstanding lean protein source: 17g protein per 100g at only 107 kcal, with just 3.5g total fat and 3g carbohydrate. Selenium content is exceptionally high at 40mcg per 100g (60mcg per 150g serving — approximately 109% of the adult RDA of 55mcg), making it one of the richest selenium sources in the Singapore hawker context. Natural cholesterol from squid is elevated (~200mg/100g) but is accompanied by minimal saturated fat (0.8g/100g), which modern cardiovascular guidelines treat favourably. Sodium is moderate at 300mg/100g (450mg per 150g serving) from the sambal paste and belacan. The dish is halal-certified, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is widely available at zi char stalls, Malay mixed rice (nasi padang) stalls, and hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia, and is a classic component of Nasi Lemak sets.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy squid profile. Micronutrient values consistent across both sources. GI is an analogous estimate using approved University of Sydney GI Database methodology for high-protein seafood dishes. Multilingual names verified for Singaporean dish requirements.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for sambal sotong as a composite cooked dish is not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy squid entry (#15149) at the dish level. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite sambal sotong dish is not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy at the prepared-dish level. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA SR Legacy squid data; no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for sambal sotong exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Cholesterol at 200mg/100g reflects squid's natural cephalopod profile and is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for mixed-species squid. Copper (1.8mg/100g), manganese (0.05mg/100g), and vitamin K (2.0mcg/100g) values are from enriched source data with confidence 0.9; these are plausible for squid-based dishes but should be verified against a dedicated micronutrient analysis if precision is required. Two default portions existed in input (150g and 120g); 150g is retained as the single default per HPB Singapore Foodo reference.
